## Workflow guidance from active subagents

Each enabled subagent below carries usage instructions for you (the dispatching agent). Read these as workflow rules that apply this iteration — they encode how to USE the subagent and when in your phase to dispatch it.

### blueprint-reviewer

- Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
  The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
  detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
  blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
  has to throw away.
- I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
  if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.

  **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
    - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
      prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
      blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
      run);
    - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
      currently under active prover work;
    - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
      live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
      dropped from objectives).

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
  with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
  flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
  a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
- I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
  directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
  chapter view is the point of running me.
- Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
  need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
  for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
  the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.

### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch

This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:

- For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
  `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
  the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
  chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
  chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
  its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
  `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
  checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
  gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
  consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
  against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
- If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
  finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
- Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
  finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
  blueprint depends on):
  1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
     same-iter fast path below).
  2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
     iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
     flagged.
  3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
     writer was asked to fix.
- **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
  the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
  scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
  must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
  `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
  gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
  prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
  still fails, F stays deferred.
- If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
  mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
  The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
  the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
  verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
  the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
  alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.

### Strategy / multi-route handling

- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
  or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
  blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
  in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
  until its blueprint coverage is in place.
- If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
  treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
  — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
  in the blueprint.

### What "deferred" means in practice

Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

  review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when

  matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with

  explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond

  descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
  deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review
  agent. A directive that asks the writer to add markers puts it in
  a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the

  reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
  `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
  need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
  are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
  parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a

  confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume

### mathlib-analogist

- I have two modes. Choose by setting `## Mode: api-alignment` or
  `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration` in the directive header.
  Dispatch the right mode for the question:

  - **api-alignment**: "should this declaration be a typeclass / a
    structure / bundled / unbundled?", "does Mathlib already do this
    and we're building a parallel?". This is the default and matches
    every trigger below under "Proactive" and "Reactive".
  - **cross-domain-inspiration**: "this categorical pattern has been
    failing for K iters, has Mathlib solved the SAME structural
    problem in a different mathematical area?". Use the cross-domain
    triggers below.

- In api-alignment mode, I tell you whether Mathlib already does the
  same thing the right way and what the cost of NOT aligning would
  be. In cross-domain-inspiration mode, I bring back structural
  analogues (with citations) and the technique each used, so you
  can port it.

### Proactive triggers (BEFORE the design ships)

Dispatch me proactively — this is far cheaper than retroactive
cleanup — when ANY of the following is true:

- You are about to write a new infrastructure definition into the
  blueprint or have a blueprint-writing subagent (per your catalog)
  write one. Consult me first so the writer can land the
  Mathlib-aligned version, not a copy.
- You are about to add a new declaration to PROGRESS.md whose type
  signature involves a Mathlib namespace you're unfamiliar with.
  Treat me as a sanity check on the signature shape.
- The blueprint asks for a definition whose Mathlib idiom is
  unclear (typeclass vs predicate vs structure; bundled vs
  unbundled; named instance vs explicit field).

### Reactive triggers (when something already went wrong)

Dispatch me when:

- Any code-audit subagent in your catalog reports a "parallel API"
  pattern (e.g. the project defines `Scheme.HModule` by copy-and-
  modify from a Mathlib AddCommGrp version).
- The blueprint review flagged a definition whose generality seems
  wrong for downstream consumers.
- **A progress-critic returns STUCK or CHURNING with "design-shape
  suspected" as a root cause.** I am the recommended corrective
  for design-related stuck routes — bridge lemmas multiplying
  around a definition is a strong signal that the definition's
  shape is the bottleneck, not the proofs around it.

### Cross-domain inspiration triggers

Dispatch me in **cross-domain-inspiration** mode when:

- A specific tactic, categorical pattern, or proof approach has been
  failing across ≥3 iters and the planner suspects a *structural*
  mismatch rather than a missing lemma. The progress-critic's
  STUCK / CHURNING verdicts with "approach-shape suspected" as a
  root cause are explicit signals.
- The strategy-critic suggested an alternative route but you don't
  have a concrete starting shape — ask me to find precedents for
  the alternative in any Mathlib area.
- A new infrastructure construction is hard and you want to see
  whether Mathlib has solved the SAME categorical / topological /
  algebraic shape in a different domain (presheaf on schemes vs
  presheaf on topological spaces; pushout in algebra vs in
  geometry; descent in algebraic geometry vs in functional analysis;
  fixed-point theorems across measure theory / order theory /
  topology; ...).

In cross-domain-inspiration mode, your directive replaces the
api-alignment fields with:

- `## Structural problem` — the categorical / algebraic / topological
  shape you're trying to solve, stated as abstractly as possible
  ("we need a sheaf condition for a locally trivializable functor of
  modules", "we need a descent statement for a stack property along
  a flat surjection", ...).
- `## Failed approaches` — one short bullet per attempt that didn't
  work, with the underlying obstacle.
- `## Search radius` — `wide` (any Mathlib domain) | `narrow` (same
  general area as the project, but a different sub-area).

My output in cross-domain-inspiration mode is a ranked list of
structural analogues — each with: the Mathlib citation, the
technique used there, and a concrete suggestion for how to port it
to the project's setting. The planner uses this as an exploration
list, not as a directive.

### Strict severity

When I find that Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project chose
a parallel API anyway, I report this as critical, even if the
project's path works. "Works" is not the bar when the cost is API
fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and downstream code that can't consume
the unified Mathlib version.

I am NOT a sanity-check stamp. Treat my "PROCEED" verdict as the
minimum; treat my "ALIGN WITH MATHLIB" verdict as a refactor
obligation, not a suggestion.

I produce a persistent file under `analogies/<slug>.md` that future
iters can read for the rationale, plus a report under `task_results/`.

### progress-critic

- I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
  do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog

  **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
    - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
      or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
      skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
    - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
      signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
      added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
      CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
    - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the

  with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on

  re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
- My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
  progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
  The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned

### Strict context discipline

Your directive must contain ONLY:
  - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
    iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
  - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
    sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
    (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.
  - For each route, the strategy's CURRENT `Iters left` estimate
    and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —
    so the critic can compare "estimated K iters" against "elapsed
    K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
    `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
    whole strategy.
  - The planner's PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives` proposal for
    this iter (file count + the basenames). Dispatch-sanity checks
    operate on this list — see "What you check" item 6.
  - K should be 3-5; more iters = better detection.

Your directive MUST NOT include:
  - STRATEGY.md (my question is convergence, not strategic
    soundness — that is the strategy critic's territory).
  - Blueprint chapters (math correctness is the blueprint
    reviewer's territory).
  - Iter sidecars' full content (just the extracted signals named
    above).

If the directive includes content I am NOT supposed to see, ignore
it. My value depends on the narrow focus.

### Acting on my verdicts

Verdicts are per-route:

- **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
  prover round.
- **CHURNING** — each iter adds helpers but the residual hasn't
  shrunk. STOP assigning more helpers. My report names the
  corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,
  structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the
  matching subagent from the catalog.
- **STUCK** — no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K
  iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.
- **UNCLEAR** — not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of
  data). Proceed but watch.

**CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must
respond — either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit
rebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.
Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the
failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.

Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).

149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.

1	# Review Agent

=== task_result ===
# AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean

## Summary (iter-156)

File compiles green (`lean_diagnostic_messages` success). Two `sorry`s remain, both
**verified-gated** on out-of-file / plan-level infrastructure; neither is closeable from
within `Jacobian.lean` this iteration. No new axioms, no bare-sorry reversion. The
substantive contribution this iter is the **precise identification and verification of the
blockers** on the genus-0 keystone `key`, recorded in the inline comment (lines ~234-262)
for the next prover/plan agent.

Note: PROGRESS.md (iter-156 close) explicitly listed `Jacobian.lean` as **off-limits this
iteration** ("gated until the AV rigidity-lemma stack is blueprinted + scaffolded"). The
findings below confirm that gate is real and pinpoint *why*.

## `genusZeroWitness` — `key : f = toUnit C ≫ η[A]` (the `isAlbaneseFor` factorisation)

Everything around `key` is already closed (6/7 structural fields + the pointed condition +
the uniqueness clause via epi-cancellation of `toUnit C`). The ONLY remaining gap is the
genus-0 rigidity equation `key`.

Goal at the sorry:
```
f : C ⟶ A,  P : 𝟙_ ⟶ C,  _hf : P ≫ f = η[A],  h : genus C = 0   (general [Field k])
⊢ f = toUnit C ≫ η[A]
```

### Attempt — wire `key` to `rigidity_over_kbar` (blueprint route C.2.f, committed route (c))
- **Approach:** base-change `(C, A, f, P)` along `Spec k̄ → Spec k`, apply
  `rigidity_over_kbar` over `k̄`, descend to `k` via `Flat.epi_of_flat_of_surjective`.
- **Result:** BLOCKED — three independent, verified gates, all out-of-file:

  1. **IMPORT CYCLE (new finding, verified).** `rigidity_over_kbar` lives in
     `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar`. Import chain: `RigidityKbar → Rigidity → Jacobian`.
     Hence `Jacobian.lean` **cannot** import `RigidityKbar` (would cycle
     `Jacobian → RigidityKbar → Rigidity → Jacobian`). Confirmed: `rigidity_over_kbar` is
     "Unknown identifier" inside `Jacobian.lean`. **Plan-level fix required**: relocate the
     rigidity-lemma stack UPSTREAM of `Jacobian` (e.g. its own file imported by `Jacobian`),
     or restate genus-0 rigidity in an importable location, before any prover can wire `key`.
  2. **CHAR-`p` LOGICAL GAP (verified).** `rigidity_over_kbar` carries `[CharZero kbar]`.
     `lean_multi_attempt` confirms `CharZero (AlgebraicClosure k)` is **unsynthesizable**
     from `[Field k]` (`inferInstance` fails — false in char `p`). So even after the import
     is fixed and the body is closed, the char-`p` arm of this `[Field k]` goal stays
     unbacked until **route (c)** (theorem-of-the-cube → rigidity lemma, Milne §I;
     `Jacobian.tex §sec:av_rigidity_route_c`) DROPS the `[CharZero]` hypothesis.
  3. **BASE-CHANGE FUNCTOR MISSING.** C.2.f needs a base-change functor
     `Over (Spec k) → Over (Spec k̄)` transferring all 7 instances
     (`SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1`, `IsProper`, `GeometricallyIrreducible`, `GrpObj`, …)
     + genus stability (`genus C_{k̄} = genus C`), then epi-cancellation. The final
     epi-cancellation step alone (`AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi_of_flat_of_surjective`) IS in
     Mathlib, but the functor + instance transfer + genus stability are not assembled in the
     project or Mathlib `b80f227`. Multi-iter sub-build.

- **Why no speculative scaffold was added:** a base-change stub would be *unconsumable*
  (import cycle blocks the `rigidity_over_kbar` call; char-`p` blocks instantiation) and
  would only inflate the sorry count past the current single, well-documented gap. The gate
  is recorded in-code instead. There is **no honest provable fragment** of `key` beyond the
  already-present structure: `f` and `toUnit C ≫ η[A]` agree only after `P ≫ (-)` (both give
  `η[A]`), and `P` is not epi, so this does not yield equality — the rigidity content is
  irreducible.

- **Dead ends (do not retry):**
  - `inferInstance : CharZero (AlgebraicClosure k)` — fails (char-`p`).
  - `import AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar` into `Jacobian.lean` — would create an import
    cycle; do not attempt.

## `positiveGenusWitness` — bare term `sorry` (line ~299)

- Route A (Picard/FGA) positive-genus witness OBJECT (`Pic⁰`, a dim-`g` abelian variety,
  required even when `C(k)=∅`). Body is `sorry` because the witness object itself does not
  exist without the FGA representability engine (Hilbert/Quot infrastructure), the project's
  single largest gated build (~6500 LOC midpoint per `analogies/m3-route-audit.md`).
- **Nothing meaningful to scaffold in-file:** the underlying scheme `Pic⁰_{C/k}` cannot be
  constructed without the engine, so any structural skeleton would be vacuous. Left as the
  honest term-`sorry` with its extensive docstring. Off-critical-path per STRATEGY.md §M3.

## Concrete forward requirements (for plan agent)

1. **Resolve the import cycle** so `genusZeroWitness` can consume genus-0 rigidity: move the
   rigidity-lemma stack (route (c): `theorem_of_the_cube`, `rigidity_theorem`,
   `rational_map_to_AV_extends`, `unirational_to_AV_constant`,
   `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV`) into a file UPSTREAM of `Jacobian.lean`. This must precede
   any prover dispatch on `key`.
2. **Drop `[CharZero]` from `rigidity_over_kbar`** via route (c) (char-free), else `key` is
   only provable for `[Field k]` of char 0 — insufficient for the protected signature.
3. **Build the base-change functor** `Over (Spec k) → Over (Spec k̄)` + instance transfer +
   genus stability (multi-iter AV/base-change sub-project) for the C.2.f descent.

## Blueprint markers
- `def:genusZeroWitness` (`AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness`): formalized with a single
  remaining `key` sorry; structural fields + uniqueness closed. Remains `\leanok` statement,
  NOT proof (sorry present) — `sync_leanok` will handle.
- `def:positiveGenusWitness`: statement formalized, body `sorry`.
- No marker changes requested.
=== PROGRESS.md (Current Objectives + off-limits) ===
# Project Progress

## Current Stage

prover

## Stages
- [x] init
- [x] autoformalize
- [ ] prover
- [ ] polish

## End-state overview

**Zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms** for Christian Merten's Jacobian
challenge. Spine = **pointed vs. unpointed** (the witness object `J` is real on
both arms; vacuity touches only the `∀ P` Albanese field for unpointed `C`). The
genus-0 rigidity content is proved **over an algebraically closed base field k̄**
and descended to a general base field `k`.

## Iter-156 = route decided + genus-0 rigidity DE-RISKED (route (c) confirmed)

The route (a)-vs-(b) question deferred from iter-155 was resolved — and the framing
overturned in the project's favour:

- **strategy-critic** caught that the genus-0 witness OBJECT is the trivial `Spec k`
  (skeleton already 6/7 closed); genus-0 needs only a rigidity STATEMENT, NOT the Pic⁰
  representability engine. It proposed route (c): a targeted char-free "Mor(ℙ¹,A)
  constant" rigidity lemma, decoupled from the riskiest A.2 representability.
- **reference-retriever** fetched Milne's *Abelian Varieties* (`references/abelian-varieties.pdf`)
  and **CONFIRMED route (c)**: theorem of the cube → Rigidity Theorem 1.1 → Thm 3.2 →
  Prop 3.10 (unirational→AV constant). This proves "map into an AV is constant" —
  exactly `rigidity_over_kbar` — **without Serre duality, without representability,
  char-free**. The `df=0` framing was a red herring of the chart route.
- **progress-critic** confirmed the differential `df=0`/chart route is STUCK
  (`rigidity_over_kbar` never closed, iters 149–155) — the pivot is warranted.

**Decision (COMMIT route (c)):** genus-0 rigidity closes via the AV rigidity-lemma

6:The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of $C$ in the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine $g$-dimensional abelian variety even when $C(k) = \emptyset$ (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$ and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus $0$ the witness object is instead the trivial scheme $\Spec k$, \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement} (\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}), whose committed proof route is the characteristic-free theorem-of-the-cube $\to$ rigidity-lemma chain of \cref{sec:av_rigidity_route_c}, \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument.
310:  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, def:genusZeroWitness, thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
311:  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-$0$ sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$ --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from $\bar k$ to the base field $k$ along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme $J$; only the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ varies with the marked point $P$. We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
350:  This route follows Milne's \emph{Abelian Varieties} (Chapter~III) and avoids FGA representability at the cost of requiring quotients of schemes by finite group actions.
397:        \item[\textbf{(c) [committed; characteristic-free]}] \emph{Theorem of the cube $\to$ rigidity lemma.} $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is a nonsingular, unirational (indeed rational) variety, so every rational map $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \dashrightarrow A$ to an abelian variety is constant by \cref{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant} (Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.10); a morphism is in particular a rational map, so $f$ is constant. The cited proposition rests on \cref{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends} (Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined), \cref{cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp} (Corollary~1.5), \cref{lem:rigidity_theorem} (the Rigidity Theorem~1.1), and \cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube} (the theorem of the cube). This chain uses \emph{neither} $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre duality \emph{nor} the Picard representability engine, and is valid in arbitrary characteristic. It is the route the project commits to for \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}; see \cref{sec:av_rigidity_route_c}.
411:      \item[\textbf{(C.2.f)}] \emph{Descent of morphism equality from $\bar k$ to $k$.} The rigidity conclusion of Sub-step~C.2.d is proved over the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$, where \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} now carries the hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$. To obtain the corresponding equality of morphisms over the base field $k$, we descend along the base change $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$.
413:      Concretely, let $f, c \colon C \to A$ be two $k$-morphisms (in the application $f$ is the given map and $c$ the constant map at $\eta_A$). Base-change along $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ produces $f_{\bar k}, c_{\bar k} \colon C_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ together with the projections $p \colon C_{\bar k} = C \times_k \bar k \to C$ and $q \colon A_{\bar k} \to A$, fitting the base-change squares $q \circ f_{\bar k} = f \circ p$ and $q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p$. Suppose $f_{\bar k} = c_{\bar k}$ (this is what \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} delivers over $\bar k$). Then $f \circ p = q \circ f_{\bar k} = q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p$, so $f$ and $c$ agree after pre-composition with $p$.
417:      \item[\textbf{(C.2.g)}] \emph{Mathlib gap statement.} The keystone fact of Sub-step~C.2.d --- that every morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ to a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$ is constant --- is \emph{not} present in current Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) in any form usable here. The project records it as the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, whose base field currently carries $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ and (pending the characteristic-free route below) $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$. The \textbf{committed} proof route for this declaration is route~(c) of C.2.d: the theorem-of-the-cube $\to$ rigidity-lemma chain (\cref{sec:av_rigidity_route_c}, headline \cref{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant}), which is characteristic-free and uses neither Serre duality nor the Picard engine, and which --- once formalised --- lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis be dropped. The differential decomposition into the shared cotangent-vanishing pile of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} pieces (i)--(iii) --- (i) cotangent triviality of a group scheme over $\bar k$ via the functorial shear iso; (ii) scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec \bar k$ via the \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass plus the iter-125-refactored \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}; (iii) characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$ --- is the \textbf{fallback} route~(a), off the critical path but retained in the tree (each piece an upstream Mathlib gap-fill in its own right; see \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}). When $g = \dim A = 0$ the statement is vacuous and the declaration's conclusion is automatic. The reduction step C.2.b reuses the project's already-formalised Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (after the iter-125 refactor, this is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, stated at the scheme level: source reduced and geometrically irreducible, target separated); the promotion step C.2.e uses standard reduced-source / separated-target lemmas, partially in Mathlib. The descent step C.2.f --- from $\bar k$ back to the base field $k$ --- is \emph{not} a Mathlib gap: it is a short ($\sim 2$-line) consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective} (already in Mathlib, in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Flat}) together with base-change-square commutativity, and is cheaper than the proper-cohomology flat-base-change machinery a cohomological descent would have required.
426:    \item Sub-step C.2 reduces, via the project's already-formalised rigidity lemma (Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} of Chapter~\ref{chap:Rigidity}), to the classical fact that proper rational curves on a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ are constant (the keystone of Sub-step~C.2.d, packaged as \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}). This classical fact is not in Mathlib in the form needed here; see Sub-step~C.2.g for the explicit gap statement and the inventory of pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile. The descent of morphism equality from $\bar k$ back to the base field $k$ (Sub-step~C.2.f) is \emph{not} a Mathlib gap: it is a short consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective} together with base-change-square commutativity (see above).
433:    \item[\textbf{($\alpha$)}] \emph{Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure plus FGA representability (COMMITTED M3 infrastructure --- iter-144 disposition).} This is the committed Mathlib build-out that unlocks Route A, which in turn discharges the positive-genus arm of the witness existence (\cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). It is \textbf{mandatory and unconditional for the positive-genus witness \emph{object}}: that object must be a genuine $g$-dimensional abelian variety ($\Pic^0_{C/k}$), and no other route in this chapter produces it. In particular the critical path here is \emph{independent of the genus-$0$ route choice} --- the genus-$0$ arm is decoupled onto the trivial object $\Spec k$ (\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}) and the rigidity statement \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}, neither of which touches the Picard engine. Route~A is the single highest-cost build-out, since Hilbert/Quot schemes are upstream of much of modern algebraic geometry; but once present, the rest of Route A (identity component, smoothness, properness, universal property --- the last being the Albanese property of $\Pic^0$, Milne \emph{Abelian Varieties} Proposition~6.1) follows by classical arguments already widely formalised in adjacent settings.
434:    \item[\textbf{($\gamma$)}] \emph{The rigidity statement for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$.} The genus-$0$ arm needs \emph{only} this rigidity \emph{statement}, \textbf{decoupled} from both the cotangent-vanishing pile and the Picard representability of ($\alpha$): its witness object is the trivial $\Spec k$, and the statement is the project's named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), currently carrying $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ (and, pending the route below, $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$). The \textbf{committed} proof route is the characteristic-free theorem-of-the-cube $\to$ rigidity-lemma chain of \cref{sec:av_rigidity_route_c} (route~(c): headline \cref{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant} = Milne Proposition~3.10), which uses neither Serre duality nor representability. The differential decomposition into the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} --- cotangent triviality of a group scheme over $\bar k$ via the functorial shear iso; scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec \bar k$ via the \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass; characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$ --- is the \textbf{fallback} route~(a), off the critical path but retained in the tree. The genus-$0$ arm of the witness existence is packaged as \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}), which over a general base field $k$ \emph{descends} the over-$\bar k$ rigidity conclusion to $k$: for each $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$ it base-changes the data $(C, A, f, P)$ along $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (genus is stable under base change for a geometrically irreducible curve, so $C_{\bar k}$ is again a genus-$0$ curve), applies \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} over $\bar k$ to conclude $f_{\bar k}$ is constant, then descends the morphism equality back to $k$ along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (Sub-step~C.2.f, via \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}). The $C(k) = \emptyset$ branch is vacuously true (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$). Independently of ($\alpha$), establishing ($\gamma$) together with the short descent step lets the genus-$0$ part of the witness be discharged unconditionally via \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}; the higher-genus part is packaged as \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness}, iter-134 scaffold) whose body closure is gated on Route A ($\alpha$). Per the iter-135 body restructure of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, the witness existence is now a case-split $\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0$ delegating to \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} respectively, converting the formerly inline \texttt{sorry} into honest delegation to the two named scaffolds.

The restructure converts the inline \texttt{sorry} on the witness-existence theorem itself into honest delegation to two named scaffolds; the witness existence is therefore conditionally established whenever both arms' bodies close (no Mathlib gap directly on the witness-existence theorem itself, only on each arm).
